intro:
Sanctification (Greek hagiasmos, from hagios, “holy”; Hebrew qādash, “to set apart”) is the work of God by which sinners, already justified, are made holy — set apart from sin to belong wholly to God and progressively conformed to the likeness of Christ. The same root that names God’s own transcendent holiness names the goal of the believer’s life: “You shall be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy” (Leviticus 19:2). Scripture speaks of sanctification in two senses — a definitive, once-for-all setting apart that happens at conversion (“you were washed, you were sanctified,” 1 Corinthians 6:11), and a progressive growth in actual holiness that continues throughout the Christian life (2 Corinthians 3:18).

In the Old Testament holiness radiates outward from God to whatever he claims — the sabbath, the tabernacle, the priesthood, and above all Israel, a nation set apart to be “a kingdom of priests and a holy nation” (Exodus 19:6). The ceremonial separations of Leviticus dramatized a deeper moral and relational reality: a holy God dwelling in the midst of his people requires a holy people. The prophets longed for the day when God himself would cleanse the heart and put his Spirit within, so that obedience would flow from transformed nature rather than external command (Ezekiel 36:25-27; Jeremiah 31:33).

The New Testament locates that fulfillment in Christ and the Spirit. Believers are sanctified through union with Jesus, who “became to us wisdom from God, righteousness and sanctification and redemption” (1 Corinthians 1:30); by his single offering “he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified” (Hebrews 10:14) — a verse holding together the finished work and the ongoing process. The means are the Word of truth (“Sanctify them in the truth; your word is truth,” John 17:17), the indwelling Spirit who puts sin to death (Romans 8:13), and the renewing of the mind in worship and community (Romans 12:1-2). Sanctification is therefore both gift and task: the believer is to “work out” salvation “with fear and trembling, for it is God who works in you, both to will and to work for his good pleasure” (Philippians 2:12-13). It is the express will of God (“this is the will of God, your sanctification,” 1 Thessalonians 4:3).

Though real, sanctification in this life is never complete; the flesh still wars against the Spirit, and the saints groan for full deliverance (Romans 7:21-25; Galatians 5:17). Its certain end is glorification — the predestined goal that believers “be conformed to the image of his Son” (Romans 8:29), made complete when “we shall see him as he is” and be like him (1 John 3:2). The God who began this good work “will bring it to completion at the day of Jesus Christ” (Philippians 1:6), so that sanctification rests finally not on human striving but on the faithfulness of the One who calls: “he who calls you is faithful; he will surely do it” (1 Thessalonians 5:24).
